generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance HeatbedActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Primary heating element for temperature regulation within the styrofoam incubator box. Implements bang-bang control logic with configurable LL/UL thresholds and H/C durations for thermal overshoot prevention. Communicates via RabbitMQ for remote activation/deactivation."
]

instance FanActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Heat distribution mechanism within the styrofoam box. Activated/deactivated via controller commands to ensure uniform temperature distribution. Implicitly follows controller logic for thermal uniformity."
]

// C3: Physical Sensing Components
instance InternalTemperatureSensor1 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Primary internal temperature sensor monitoring the styrofoam box environment. Measures real-time temperature for control feedback and anomaly detection."
    DTDFVocab:producedData InternalTemperatureData
]

instance InternalTemperatureSensor2 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Secondary internal temperature sensor providing redundant measurement for temperature uniformity validation and cross-checking with InternalTemperatureSensor1."
    DTDFVocab:producedData InternalTemperatureData
]

instance ExternalTemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Ambient temperature sensor monitoring room conditions outside the incubator box. Provides reference data for historical vs real-time visualization overlays."
    DTDFVocab:producedData ExternalTemperatureData
]

// C4: Physical-to-Virtual Data Transmission
instance InternalTemperatureData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InternalTemperatureSensor1, InternalTemperatureSensor2
]

instance ExternalTemperatureData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom ExternalTemperatureSensor
]

instance ControllerStateData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom BangBangController
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance ModelA : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Simplified thermal model with two free parameters approximating system magnitude and time scale. Used as baseline for Model B calibration."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo RabbitMQEnabler, TimeSeriesDatabaseEnabler
]

instance ModelB : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Advanced thermal model with four free parameters providing accurate thermal inertia prediction of the heatbed. Used for anomaly detection, state estimation, and what-if simulations."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo RabbitMQEnabler, KalmanFilterEnabler, CoSimulationEnabler
]

instance TemperatureMeasurementData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated temperature data from all sensors including internal and external measurements. Used for visualization, calibration, and anomaly detection."
    DTDFVocab:fromData InternalTemperatureData, ExternalTemperatureData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo RealTimeVisualizationService, AnomalyDetectionService
]

instance ControllerParameterData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Configuration parameters for the bang-bang controller including LL, UL, H, and C values. Used for controller reconfiguration and what-if simulations."
    DTDFVocab:fromData ControllerStateData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SelfAdaptationService, WhatIfSimulationService
]

// C11: Enablers
instance RabbitMQEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Open-source message broker facilitating decoupled communication between Physical Twin components (sensors, actuators, controller) and Digital Twin services. Enables real-time data relay and event-driven processing."
    DTDFVocab:enables RealTimeVisualizationService, OnDemandCalibrationService, AnomalyDetectionService, WhatIfSimulationService, SelfAdaptationService
]

instance TimeSeriesDatabaseEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Time-series database storing historical and real-time temperature measurements for visualization and analysis. Supports overlay visualization of historical vs real-time data."
    DTDFVocab:enables RealTimeVisualizationService
]

instance KalmanFilterEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Bayesian estimation algorithm implementing Kalman filter for noisy sensor data processing. Correlates measured data with unmeasured heatbed temperature for state estimation and anomaly detection."
    DTDFVocab:enables AnomalyDetectionService, SelfAdaptationService
]

instance CoSimulationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Co-simulation framework relaying historical/real-time Physical Twin data to Model B for scenario analysis. Enables what-if simulations under hypothetical conditions."
    DTDFVocab:enables WhatIfSimulationService
]

instance CalibrationAlgorithmEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Automated calibration framework updating Model B parameters using real-time sensor data. Implements algorithms described in Section 3.3 for improved thermal inertia prediction."
    DTDFVocab:enables OnDemandCalibrationService, SelfAdaptationService
]

// C6: Services
instance RealTimeVisualizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Real-time dashboard service visualizing incubator status including temperature trends, control states, and anomalies. Supports historical vs real-time overlays for comparative analysis."
    DTDFVocab:provides TemperatureVisualizationInsight, ControlStateVisualizationInsight
]

instance OnDemandCalibrationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "On-demand calibration service automatically initiated or via user request. Runs calibration algorithms to update Model B parameters using real-time sensor data for improved accuracy."
    DTDFVocab:provides ModelAccuracyImprovementAction, ParameterUpdateInsight
]

instance AnomalyDetectionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Anomaly detection service using Kalman filter to identify deviations from expected behavior. Estimates hidden states like heatbed thermal conditions and flags anomalies for review."
    DTDFVocab:provides AnomalyAlertInsight, StateEstimationInsight
]

instance WhatIfSimulationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "What-if simulation service using co-simulation framework to test controller responses under hypothetical scenarios. Optimizes controller parameters (LL, UL, H, C) for environmental changes."
    DTDFVocab:provides ScenarioAnalysisInsight, ParameterOptimizationAction
]

instance SelfAdaptationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Closed-loop self-adaptation service implementing a 7-step workflow for autonomous controller reconfiguration. Adapts to changing operational conditions like adding cold water to the incubator."
    DTDFVocab:provides AutonomousAdaptationAction, ControllerOptimizationInsight
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance TemperatureVisualizationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time visualization of internal and external temperature trends with configurable overlay of historical data for comparative analysis."
]

instance ControlStateVisualizationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Dashboard display of current control states including heatbed/fan activation status and anomaly flags."
]

instance ModelAccuracyImprovementAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated update of Model B parameters to improve thermal inertia prediction accuracy."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance ParameterUpdateInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Notification of successful parameter calibration with confidence metrics for updated Model B accuracy."
]

instance AnomalyAlertInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time alerts for detected deviations from expected behavior including sudden temperature drops or unexpected control state changes."
]

instance StateEstimationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Estimated values for hidden states like heatbed temperature derived from Kalman filter processing of sensor data."
]

instance ScenarioAnalysisInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Predicted behavior scenarios under hypothetical conditions including environmental changes or controller parameter variations."
]

instance ParameterOptimizationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated adjustment of controller parameters (LL, UL, H, C) based on what-if simulation results."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance AutonomousAdaptationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Full closed-loop workflow executing anomaly detection, data collection, controller reconfiguration, and model recalibration for autonomous adaptation."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance ControllerOptimizationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Optimized controller parameters validated through what-if simulations and ready for physical deployment."
]

// C1: Environment
instance IncubatorEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:desc "Thermal incubator system within an insulated styrofoam box environment. Contains all physical components and serves as the operational domain for temperature regulation."
    base:contains HeatbedActuator, FanActuator, InternalTemperatureSensor1, InternalTemperatureSensor2, ExternalTemperatureSensor, BangBangController, LowLevelDriver
]

// C5: Components
instance BangBangController : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Raspberry Pi-based bang-bang controller implementing configurable control logic with LL/UL thresholds, H heating duration, and C waiting duration. Enforces safety protocols and communicates via RabbitMQ."
]

instance LowLevelDriver : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Safety-critical abstraction layer enforcing safe operational states and periodic temperature validation. Periodically reads sensor data and uploads state updates to RabbitMQ."
]


instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "
    **Control Flows from Digital to Physical:**
    - **Command Types:**
      - Heating commands (activate/deactivate heatbed)
      - Fan control commands (activate/deactivate fan)
      - Temperature setpoints (reference values for control logic)

    - **Control Message Formats:**
      - Asynchronous messages via RabbitMQ (no explicit format specified, but implied to be JSON or similar lightweight formats)
      - Controller statecharts (e.g., bang-bang control logic in Document 6) define command sequences

    - **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms:**
      - No explicit validation/authentication mentioned
      - Safe protocol incorporated in low-level driver (Document 6) to prevent unsafe states

    - **Feedback Systems:**
      - Closed-loop control via temperature sensor feedback (continuous monitoring)
      - Kalman filter integration (Document 5) for state estimation (e.g., heatbed temperature)

    - **Conflict Resolution:**
      - Not explicitly addressed, but implied to rely on controller logic (e.g., waiting durations in bang-bang control)

    - **Command Execution Confirmation:**
      - Low-level driver acknowledges commands via RabbitMQ (implicit feedback loop)
      - Temperature readings confirm command effects (e.g., heating response)
  "
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "
    - **Real-time visualization**: Implemented via periodic data collection from the controller and low-level driver messages, relayed to a time-series database with unspecified but implied periodic frequency (documented in **Document 5**).
    - **Temperature monitoring**: The low-level driver periodically reads temperature data (frequency not specified but implied to be periodic; **Document 6**).
    - **Controller operation**: Bang-bang control logic with explicit timing parameters:
      - **H (Heating duration)**: Duration of heatbed activation (unspecified exact value but critical for calibration).
      - **C (Waiting duration)**: Delay after actuation to prevent overshoot (unspecified exact value but critical for stability).
    - **Calibration process**: Triggered on-demand (documented in **Document 5**) with no explicit frequency but tied to anomaly detection events.
    - **Kalman filter execution**: Runs continuously to estimate heatbed state (latency not specified but implied to be real-time; **Document 5**).
    - **What-if simulations**: Co-simulations with historical/real-time data (frequency unspecified but tied to controller reconfiguration events; **Document 5**).
    - **Self-adaptation loop**: Event-driven with explicit steps (e.g., anomaly detection → data collection → calibration → reconfiguration) but no fixed temporal constraints (documented in **Document 5**).
    - **Latency requirements**: Implicitly low-latency for control loop (e.g., 'periodically reads' in **Document 6**), but no explicit numerical bounds provided.
  "
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "
    **Orchestration and Coordination Architecture:**
    - **Component Integration Pattern:** Follows a **pub-sub (publish-subscribe) architecture** with RabbitMQ as the central message broker. Components publish data (e.g., sensor readings, controller states) and subscribe to relevant topics (e.g., commands for actuators).
    - **Resource Management:**
      - **RabbitMQ:** Manages message queues, QoS policies, and ensures ordered delivery of commands/data between components.
      - **Controller:** Allocates resources for heating/waiting cycles (parameters **H, C**) and manages actuator states.
      - **Low-Level Driver:** Periodically reads sensor data and uploads it to RabbitMQ, ensuring **safe state protocols** (e.g., preventing unsafe temperature spikes).
    - **System-Wide Coordination:**
      - **Temporal Coordination:** Achieved via RabbitMQ’s message timestamps and QoS policies to ensure **sequential processing** of commands (e.g., heating → waiting cycles).
      - **State Synchronization:** The DT maintains a **consistent state** with the PT by:
        1. Listening to controller/driver messages via RabbitMQ.
        2. Running **Kalman filters** to correlate sensor data with model predictions.
        3. Triggering **on-demand calibration** when anomalies are detected.
      - **Adaptation Loop:** Implemented as a **closed-loop process** where the DT:
        - Detects PT behavior changes (via Kalman filter).
        - Schedules experiments (e.g., cooling/heating ramps).
        - Reconfigures the controller and Kalman filter parameters dynamically.

    **Technology Stack:**
    - **Communication:** RabbitMQ (open-source message broker) for **asynchronous, decoupled communication** between components.
    - **Simulation:** Two dynamic models (Model A/B) implemented in **Python/MATLAB-like frameworks** (not explicitly stated but implied by heat equation derivations).
    - **Visualization:** Time-series data stored in a **database** (type unspecified) and visualized via a **dashboard** (tools not specified but likely Grafana/InfluxDB).
    - **Control:** Bang-bang controller with **statechart-based logic** (Figure 8) for temperature regulation.

    **Architecture Patterns:**
    - **Event-Driven:** Components react to events (e.g., temperature readings, commands) via RabbitMQ.
    - **Model-Driven:** The DT relies on **two calibrated models (A/B)** to predict PT behavior.
    - **Hybrid (Simulation + Monitoring):** Combines **real-time monitoring** (sensors) with **predictive simulation** (what-if scenarios).
  "
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
    **Lifecycle Stages of the Digital Twin (Incubator Case Study):**

    1. **Development Phase:**
       - **Conceptualization:** Defined as a framework integrating modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation (Documents 1, 4).
       - **Model Creation:** Two simulation models developed for temperature prediction (Document 3):
         - **Model A:** Two free parameters (simpler approximation of system magnitude/time scale).
         - **Model B:** Four free parameters (better thermal inertia prediction, preferred for DT development).
       - **Hardware/Software Setup:** Physical Twin (PT) includes styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, and temperature sensors (Documents 2, 3). Cyber components include Raspberry Pi controller, RabbitMQ communication server, and low-level driver (Documents 2, 6).
       - **Integration:** Models integrated with real-time data streams from sensors (Documents 1, 5).

    2. **Representation Types:**
       - **Physical Twin (PT):** Hardware components (insulated box, sensors, actuators) with real-time monitoring (Documents 2, 6).
       - **Cyber Twin (CT):** Simulation models (Model A/B), calibration algorithms, and data processing pipelines (Documents 1, 3, 5).
       - **Digital Twin (DT):** Hybrid system combining PT, CT, and algorithms (e.g., Kalman filter, what-if simulations) for closed-loop operation (Documents 1, 5).

    3. **Transition Management:**
       - **Calibration:** Dynamic updates to models using real-time data (e.g., Model B recalibration post-experiment) (Documents 3, 5).
       - **Self-Adaptation Loop:** Automated workflow for detecting anomalies (Kalman filter), gathering new data, recalibrating models, and reconfiguring controllers (Document 5).
       - **Version Control:** Open-source hardware/software specifications available via GitHub repository ([INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) (Documents 2, 3, 5).

    4. **Lifecycle Governance:**
       - **Real-Time Visualization:** Data relayed to time-series databases (e.g., InfluxDB implied) and dashboards for monitoring (Document 5).
       - **On-Demand Calibration:** Triggered by anomalies or environmental changes (e.g., introducing cold water) (Document 5).
       - **Continuous Evolution:** Iterative improvement via what-if simulations and controller parameter optimization (Document 5).
       - **Safety Protocols:** Low-level driver includes safeguards to prevent unsafe states (Document 6).

    **Key Technologies/Tools:**
    - **Communication:** RabbitMQ (message broker for PT-DT interaction) (Document 6).
    - **Simulation:** Custom thermal models (Model A/B) implemented in Python/Java (inferred from context).
    - **Monitoring:** Kalman filter for state estimation (Document 5).
    - **Control:** Bang-bang controller with configurable parameters (LL, UL, H, C) (Document 6).
  "
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
  **Model Fidelity Requirements:**
  - **Thermal Model Accuracy:** Model B (4 parameters) outperforms Model A (2 parameters) in predicting heatbed inertia (Section 3).
  - **Dynamic Response:** Models must accurately replicate PT behavior under varying loads (e.g., adding cold objects).
  - **Control Fidelity:** Bang-bang controller parameters (LL, UL, H, C) must align with PT dynamics to avoid overshoot/undershoot.

  **Validation and Verification Methods:**
  - **Calibration Experiments:** Automated process to adjust Model B parameters using PT sensor data (Section 3.3).
  - **Anomaly Detection:** Kalman filter validates sensor data against model predictions (Section 5).
  - **Co-Simulation:** Historical/real-time data compared against model outputs for validation (Section 5).
  - **What-If Simulations:** Controller parameters tested virtually before deployment (Section 5).

  **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification:**
  - **Sensor Noise:** Implicit in temperature measurements (not quantified).
  - **Model Approximation:** Trade-off between accuracy (Model B) and computational cost (simulation speed).
  - **Environmental Variability:** External temperature fluctuations (purple line in Figure 13) affect PT behavior.
  - **Parameter Drift:** Model B recalibration required when PT behavior changes (e.g., new objects added).

  **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms:**
  - **Kalman Filter:** Estimates heatbed temperature (non-measured) and detects anomalies (Section 5).
  - **Self-Adaptation Loop:** Triggers recalibration and controller reconfiguration (Section 5):
    1. Detects anomalies via Kalman filter.
    2. Gathers new experimental data.
    3. Recalibrates Model B.
    4. Updates Kalman filter parameters.
    5. Optimizes controller via what-if simulations.
  - **Safety Protocols:** Low-level driver enforces safe states (Section 2.2.2).

  **Quality Assurance Processes:**
  - **Open-Source Validation:** Hardware/software specifications available for community review (GitHub repository [6]).
  - **Experimental Reproducibility:** Calibration results (Figure 13) and datasets publicly accessible.
  - **Iterative Improvement:** Future work includes refining self-adaptation loop (Section 5).
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
    **Technical Network Infrastructure:**
    - **Communication Protocols:**
      - RabbitMQ (AMQP protocol) for message brokering
      - TCP/IP for underlying network communication

    - **Network Topology and Architecture:**
      - Centralized architecture with RabbitMQ as the message broker
      - Raspberry Pi (controller) and low-level driver communicate via RabbitMQ
      - No explicit mention of distributed or peer-to-peer topologies

    - **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements:**
      - Not explicitly quantified, but implied to be low-latency (<100ms for control loops, inferred from real-time requirements)
      - RabbitMQ is lightweight and suitable for edge/on-premise deployments

    - **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms:**
      - No explicit redundancy mechanisms mentioned
      - RabbitMQ supports persistence and clustering for reliability

    - **Security Protocols:**
      - No explicit security protocols mentioned (e.g., TLS, authentication)
      - Safe protocol in low-level driver (Document 6) implies basic safeguards
  "
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
    **Deployment Infrastructure:**
    - **Hosting Platforms:**
      - On-premise (Raspberry Pi + RabbitMQ)
      - Cloud deployment options for RabbitMQ (Document 6)

    - **Computational Requirements:**
      - Raspberry Pi (low-power embedded device) for controller and low-level driver
      - No explicit CPU/GPU requirements for DT components mentioned

    - **Storage Requirements:**
      - Time-series database for storing temperature data (implied for visualization)
      - No explicit storage quotas or formats mentioned

    - **Scalability Mechanisms:**
      - RabbitMQ supports horizontal scaling (clustering)
      - No explicit scaling strategies for DT components mentioned

    - **Deployment Models Supported:**
      - Edge deployment (Raspberry Pi + RabbitMQ on-premise)
      - Hybrid (on-premise + cloud for RabbitMQ)

    - **Availability/Reliability Assurance:**
      - No explicit SLAs or redundancy mechanisms mentioned
      - RabbitMQ persistence ensures data availability during restarts
  "
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "
    **Integration with External Systems:**
    - **Data Exchange Protocols:**
      - **RabbitMQ (AMQP protocol):** Used for internal communication between PT components (controller, low-level driver) and DT services. No mention of external protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) for integration with enterprise systems.
      - **Time-Series Database:** Implied for storing sensor data and visualization (protocol not specified).
    - **Interoperability Standards:**
      - **No explicit standards** mentioned for horizontal integration (e.g., no mention of OPC UA, DDS, or REST APIs for external systems).
      - **Open-Source Hardware/Software:** The incubator’s hardware/software is available on GitHub ([link](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)), suggesting potential for **custom integration** with other systems.
    - **Dependencies on External Systems:**
      - **None explicitly mentioned.** The DT operates in isolation for this case study, with no dependencies on external IoT platforms, cloud services, or legacy systems.
    - **Integration Patterns:**
      - **Decoupled Communication:** RabbitMQ enables **loose coupling** between PT and DT components, allowing future integration with external systems via RabbitMQ plugins or APIs.
      - **Data-Driven Integration:** The DT’s visualization and simulation capabilities could theoretically integrate with external dashboards or analytics tools by exposing the time-series database via APIs (not implemented in this case study).
      - **No Federated DTs:** No mention of integrating with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems (e.g., PLM, MES). The DT is **standalone** for this use case.

    **Potential Integration Scenarios (Implied):**
    - **Cloud Integration:** RabbitMQ supports cloud deployment, enabling integration with cloud-based analytics or storage (e.g., AWS S3, Google BigQuery).
    - **Edge Computing:** The Raspberry Pi-based controller could integrate with edge gateways for distributed processing (not addressed in the documents).
    - **Legacy System Integration:** Custom adapters could bridge RabbitMQ with legacy SCADA systems or ERP platforms (e.g., via REST or SOAP APIs).
  "
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
  **Standards Compliance & Interoperability:**
  - **Technologies Adopted:**
    - **RabbitMQ:** Open-source message broker for communication (Document 6, Section 2.2.1).
    - **GitHub:** Version control for hardware/software specs (Document 2, 5).
    - **Open-Source Tools:** All components publicly available (Document 2, 5).

  - **Related Research Frameworks:**
    - **Models@Runtime:** DT aligns with runtime model adaptation concepts (Document 2).
    - **Self-Adaptive Systems:** DT includes self-adaptation loops (Document 2, 5).

  - **Lack of Formal Certification:**
    - No explicit mention of compliance with IEEE, ISO, or other industry standards (Document 2, 6).
    - No certification requirements or frameworks referenced.

  - **Interoperability Standards:**
    - **Protocol:** RabbitMQ enables interoperability between PT and DT components (Document 6).
    - **Data Formats:** Time-series data for visualization (Document 5).
  "
]
